system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 2: How to Memorize Chords

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 2 — How to Memorize Chords

_Pages 10-11._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## O notation (pluck after strum)

> The symboi (1 indicates a note to be played afteran x. Example: 7
>
> 3 fp
> 7 __ aoa
>
> As with x’s, if more than one O appears, play them together (after the x’s).

**Significance (p. 10).** This defines the 'O' diagram symbol as a note plucked after the strummed x-notes, establishing the sequencing rule for reading chord diagrams in this method.

## Chord name flexibility with x/O

> The naming of chords with x’s is really debatable. For instance:
>
> kom? kemTfl
> on
>
> pie ora
>
> Both approaches are fine and you will see both of them used, so don’t expect
> consistency here. Why? Because as you will see, there are times when one
> approach would be ridiculous, and vice versa.

**Significance (p. 10).** This gives an explicit rule that chord naming with x's is intentionally inconsistent, alerting the reader not to treat one naming convention as authoritative.

## Chord symbol notation rules

> a) a slash through a number means major (Example: #= Major 7th); b) + means sharped 5th unless it comes before 11
> (+11) in which case it means sharped 11th; c) sus.is an abbreviation for the word suspended which means that the 3rd
>
> has been replaced with the 4th (it also has another meaning but you needn’t worry about it in this book).

**Significance (p. 10).** This defines three critical chord symbol conventions—major slash notation, augmented/sharp-11 disambiguation, and sus—that are essential for reading chord names throughout the book.

## Visual chord memorization method

> Many guitar players learn new chords quickly by relating them visually to chords they already know. Example:
> Suppose you encounter the following chord for the first time and after playing it, you
> a
>
> decide that you like the sound enough to want to memorize it. Suggestion: try relating it visually to:

**Significance (p. 10).** This introduces the first memorization strategy—anchoring new chord shapes to visually similar known chords—which is the method's primary technique for building chord vocabulary.

## Five neck areas for chord memorization

> Divide the neck up into five main areas for any major or minor chord and use these areas as reference points for
> any chord that you learn. For instance, using the A major chord as a model, the five areas would be:

**Significance (p. 10).** This introduces the five-position neck framework—a foundational concept in this method for organizing and memorizing all major and minor chord shapes across the fretboard.

## Fingerings apply to initial chord only

> If left hand fingerings are
> indicated in a diagrarm which contains x’s or 01’s, these fingerings indicate the initial chord sound only unless otherwise
> indicated.

**Significance (p. 10).** This rule clarifies that fingering numbers in mixed diagrams govern only the first strummed chord voicing, preventing misapplication of fingerings to subsequent plucked notes.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
